noteGrp.note: The MS. has , not . as L. prints.
MS. , not , as L.
The word has dropped out before , as appears from the heading of the chapter below.
vii. 14.
vii. 14; Mich. fol. 161 r; cf. Jo. Mal. p. 410.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
δημόσιον.
κάστρον.
Cf. Jo. Mal. pp. 410, 411.
πραιπόσιτος, i.e. præpositus sacri cubiculli.
ῥόγας.
Mich. loc. cit.
Cf. Jo. Mal. loc. cit.
Cf. Theoph. A.M. 6012.
Cf. Evag. iv. 4.
vii. 13.
στρατηγός.
There is no before , as L., but the letter must be inserted.

. The usual meaning of this word is "take,'' which is here impossible. The word sometimes, however, means "shut," and the rendering in the text seems justified by the use in vii. 5 (p. 212, l. 23, L.).
Cf. Jo. Mal. p. 412; Evag. iv. 3.
It is very probable that we should insert before , translating, "various tribes also and Goths following him, he came confidently.''
στρατηγός.

In bk. 3, ch. 4 this word means "godfather"; but, since this meaning seems to be here out of place, I have given it the indefinite rendering above. The relation meant is that of the σύντεκνος, which we can hardly express in English (Smith, Thesaurus, col. 4342).
Cf. Evag. iv. 4.
στρατηγός.

There is probably some mistake in the heading, as a bishop could not be an apokrisiarios. Jo. Eph. (ap. "Dion.") omits the word, while Mart. Areth. speaks of a Συμεωνίτου πρεσβυτέρου καὶ ἀποκρισιαρίου.

This chapter is contained in Cod. Rom. Mai's text is, however, not taken from the MS. but is a copy of that of Assemani (B. O. vol. i. p. 364 ff.), taken not from our author, but from John of Ephesus (ap. "Dion."). The letter also exists in a much longer form in Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,650, fol. 155, and in a MS. in the Museum Borgianum at Rome, which has been edited by Prof. Guidi [Atti dell’ Accademia de’ Lincei, Ser. 3, Tom. 7, 1881). It exists also in Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,641, fol. 157, where the text is not a copy of 14,650, as Guidi states on the authority of Wright, but is similar to that in our author and in Jo. Eph., and, being followed by the later history of the little boy, as in Jo. Eph. , is plainly derived from that author. A Greek account of the same events, derived in part from this letter, is contained in the Martyrium Arethæ (Boissonade, Anecd. Græc. vol. v.), and a slightly different one in Simeon Metaphrastes (Migne, Patr. Græc. 115, p. 1249 ff.).
Jo. Eph. ap. "Dion." (Assem., B. O. p. 364 ff.); Mich. fol. 166 ff.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. (marg.), Jo. Eph., 14,650, and Cod. Borg.
I.e. emigrants or nomads, a name applied to certain Arab tribes: Ar. Maʿaddiyya.
Read for , with Cod. Rom., “Dion.” 14,650, and Cod. Borg. 14,641 agrees with 17,202.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints. So Cod. Rom., which, however, has instead of .

Insert before , with Cod. Rom., "Dion.," 14,650, and Cod. Borg. 14,641 agrees with 17,202.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Insert after , with Jo. Eph. 14,650 and Cod. Borg. have , "my face."
Read for , with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph.
Omit before with Cod. Rom., 14,650, Cod. Borg., and "Dion."
Insert before , with Cod. Rom., "Dion.,'' 14,650, and Cod. Borg.
The MS. has , not as L. prints.

Insert before , with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph.

The MS. has before , which L. does not print.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Insert before , with Cod. Rom. and 14,641. "Dion." has .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph.

. This word occurs again in 1. 7 (p. 73, l. 7, L.) and 9. 3 (p. 257, l. 14), where the meaning seems to be as above, not "vafer fuit," as Payne Smith. Cf. also 3. 1 (p. 123, l. 12) and 3. 10 (p. 130, l. 22). In 7. 2 (p. 203, l. 3) , from the cognate , seems to bear the same meaning.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. Jo. Eph. has
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Omit before , and read for , with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph. 14,650 and Cod. Borg. also give the same sense.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
οὐσία.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
After there follow in Jo. Eph., 14,650, and Cod. Borg. the words , "He said to him, 'By our Lord, my mother.'" This answer (absent in both MSS.) seems to have been accidentally omitted by our author.
Insert CTLJi after ;io|o, with Cod. Rom., Jo. Eph., 14,650, and Cod. Borg.
, So Jo. Eph. (not , as Assem. prints) and Mich. ().
Insert after , with Cod. Rom., Jo. Eph., 14,650, and Cod. Borg.
The MS. has not , as L. prints.
Omit before with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. and Jo. Eph.
I.e. "son of sailors."
ὕπαρχος.
Here the sentence breaks off, being taken up again lower down.
We should probably insert another in order to express the pluperfect, which is necessary for the sense, since a bishop could not be a synkellos.
Prov. x. 14.
Mich. fol. 161 r, 164 f.
Mich. fol. 164.
1 Kings ix. 18, 19.
The word has dropped out of the text, and must be supplied from the heading. The verb is also omitted in Mich.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
MS. , not , as L.
Mich. fol. 165; Greg. H. E. i. p. 201.
κόμης ἀνατολῆς.

Two leaves in the MS. have here been transposed. I pass on from p. 244, l. 26 (Land) to p. 246, l. 16. The intermediate portion has nothing to do with the subject of this chapter, but belongs to the next. This is also evident from a comparison with Michael and Gregory.
Insert before .
Mich. fol. 164 r; Greg. p. 78.
ἀνάλωμα.
τράκτατον.
The name meant is Sphahpat (Ἀσπεβέδης), the title of the Persian commander-in-chief (Josh. Styl. 59, and Wright’s note). Cf. also 9. 4.
βερηδάριοι.
Omit before .

seems to be corrupt. There is no authority for the form for Emesa, and only a few lines above our author writes ; but it may be an accidental corruption. Mich. has (δήμιος?), but a name of a town seems to be required.
Mich. fol. 162 r.
Jo. Eph. ap. Mich. fol. 161 v, Greg. H. E. pp. 195, 197, from Philox. Ep. ad Mon. Sen. (Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,597, fol. 35 ff.).
ξενοδοχεῖον.
Read for , with Greg. and Mich. So also Philoxenus himself (fol. 89 v).
Cf. Jo. Eph. l.c.

I cannot identify this place. should stand for Amorium; but, as it is mentioned among the Mesopotamian sees, this is out of the question. John of is mentioned among the Mesopotamian bishops in Mich. fol. 161 v. In a chronicle of the ninth cent. in Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,642, fol. 29 r, he is called Thomas of , as here; cf. Assem., B. O. 2, Diss. de Monoph. lxiii.
Mich. fol. 162 r.
Jo. Eph. ap. "Dion." (Assem., B. O. 2, pp. 48, 49); Mich. l.c.
Read for . The former form occurs twice lower down.
ἡγεμών.
βερεδάριοι.
Logically, we require , "ill," for , "illness"; but the meaning is clear.
At this point we go back to fol. 140 (see p. 205, note 6).
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for .
. This seems to represent ἀπὸ δικανικῶν, as in 7. 10; but the meaning does not suit this passage very well.
Not Aphthonius; see John's life in Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 12,174, fol. 84.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has before ., not , as L. prints.
2 Cor. i. 4(?); 1 Thess. iii. 7(?).
In Jo. Eph. (Land, Anecd. Syr. ii. p. 110, l. 3) "Beth Shurʿo." "Dion." "in the temple of Beth Shilo."
Mich. fol. 163.
Mich. "was expelled together with the brotherhood, and the monks of the monastery…"
Of I can make nothing, and take the meaning from Mich. ). Read either or . Dr. Hamilton proposes , "that of my lord Romanus."
Read for . Cf. Wright, C. B. M. pp. 605, 755, etc.; Mich. .
Text, "Magos"; so Mich.
The last letter of this word, left blank by L., is .
See bk. 7, ch. 4 (p. 156, note 2).
Or, "near Harmosho." I find no trace of either of these names, and suspect both to be corrupt. Mich, omits both, writing merely "Sergius, and the monastery… Hauro."
At this point we return to fol. 142.
Mich. has the masculine, "who is now polluted."
πολιτεία.
Sir. xxix. 21.
I.e. learned in the inner or allegorical meaning.
Isa. xxvi. 20.
Cf. Mich. fol. 167 v.
We should probably read , "book," for .

This chapter is contained in Cod. Rom., where the heading is, "The prologue composed concisely under heads by the holy Moro, bishop of Amida, a man deserving of blessed memory, upon the Gospel and the dispensation of Christ in the flesh."—Cf. p. 221.
Mich. fol. 162 ff.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints, before .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
θεωρία.
Instead of , Cod. Rom. has , "repeated." Mich. agrees with Cod. Brit.
After insert , with Cod. Rom. So Mich.

Cod. Rom. seems to have , not , as Mai prints; and, as Mich. also has "made," this must be the right word. The participle, however, cannot here stand alone, so that we must either read or suppose to have dropped out.
John iii. 31.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Insert before , with Cod. Rom.
Cod. Rom., "that it is now in His power.''

Insert before , with Cod. Rom. So. Mich.
John viii. 1-11.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.
Cod. Rom., like Cod. Brit., has , not , as Mai prints.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. L. prints , but there is no point either above or below the letter in the MS.
Cod. Rom. has as Cod. Brit., not as Mai prints.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. The reading of Cod. Brit, is doubtful.
